card.indications: Adjunctive treatment of open-angle and secondary glaucoma; temporary reduction of intraocular pressure before surgery in acute angle-closure glaucoma; prevention or reduction of acute mountain sickness symptoms; adjunctive treatment of selected seizure disorders; and treatment of edema related to heart failure or medications.
card.mechanism: Inhibits carbonic anhydrase in the renal tubules, ciliary body, and other tissues. This increases renal bicarbonate, sodium, and water excretion, causes mild metabolic acidosis, decreases aqueous-humor formation and intraocular pressure, and may reduce abnormal neuronal firing and cerebrospinal-fluid production.
card.dosing: Dosing varies by indication and formulation. Glaucoma regimens commonly use 250 mg one to four times daily or an extended-release formulation according to the prescription. Acute mountain sickness prevention commonly uses 500 to 1,000 mg/day in divided doses beginning before ascent. Edema and seizure dosing must be individualized. Reduce or avoid dosing in renal impairment and follow product-specific limits.
card.routeAdministration: Give oral doses with or without food and at consistent times. Extended-release capsules should be swallowed whole. IV powder must be reconstituted and administered according to product instructions. For edema, intermittent dosing may preserve effectiveness better than uninterrupted daily use. Do not substitute formulations without confirming the ordered dose.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to acetazolamide or formulation ingredients; depressed serum sodium or potassium; marked kidney or liver disease; cirrhosis because of hepatic-encephalopathy risk; adrenal insufficiency; hyperchloremic acidosis; and long-term use in chronic noncongestive angle-closure glaucoma.
card.warnings: Rare fatal sulfonamide-type reactions may include Stevens-Johnson syndrome, toxic epidermal necrolysis, aplastic anemia, agranulocytosis, fulminant hepatic necrosis, and anaphylaxis. Metabolic acidosis, hyponatremia, hypokalemia, kidney stones, and worsening respiratory acidosis may occur. High-dose aspirin with acetazolamide has caused severe toxicity, coma, and death.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Tingling of the fingers, toes, or face; increased urination; fatigue; drowsiness; dizziness; altered taste, especially with carbonated beverages; nausea; vomiting; diarrhea; decreased appetite; and mild electrolyte changes.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe metabolic acidosis or electrolyte depletion; Stevens-Johnson syndrome or toxic epidermal necrolysis; aplastic anemia, agranulocytosis, or thrombocytopenia; anaphylaxis; kidney stones or acute kidney injury; hepatic encephalopathy; severe neurologic changes; and suicidal thoughts or behavior associated with antiseizure therapy.
card.interactions: High-dose aspirin can markedly increase toxicity. Sodium bicarbonate increases kidney-stone risk. Acetazolamide may alter concentrations or effects of phenytoin, primidone, lithium, amphetamines, quinidine, and other drugs affected by urinary pH. Other diuretics or medications that lower sodium, potassium, or bicarbonate increase electrolyte risk.
card.monitoring: Obtain baseline and periodic serum electrolytes, bicarbonate, renal function, and CBC with platelets. Assess hydration, intake and output, neurologic status, respiratory status in patients with pulmonary disease, and signs of acidosis. Monitor intraocular pressure, edema, seizure control, or altitude symptoms according to the indication.
card.patientTeaching: Report rash, blistering, fever, sore throat, unusual bruising, severe weakness, confusion, rapid breathing, reduced urine output, flank pain, or vision changes immediately. Tingling and increased urination are common. Avoid driving until effects are known. Do not take high-dose aspirin or sodium bicarbonate products unless specifically approved.
card.nutrition: May be taken with food if stomach upset occurs. Maintain adequate hydration unless fluids are restricted, because dehydration and kidney stones are possible. Follow prescribed potassium and sodium guidance rather than self-supplementing. Carbonated beverages may taste unusual during therapy.

card.indications: Treatment of multiple inflammatory autoimmune conditions, including rheumatoid arthritis, juvenile idiopathic arthritis, psoriatic arthritis, ankylosing spondylitis, Crohn disease, ulcerative colitis, plaque psoriasis, hidradenitis suppurativa, and noninfectious uveitis. Approved indications vary by product and age.
card.mechanism: Binds to tumor necrosis factor alpha and prevents it from activating cell-surface TNF receptors. This reduces inflammatory signaling, immune-cell recruitment, joint and tissue injury, and other manifestations of TNF-mediated disease.
card.dosing: Dosing depends on the condition, age, weight, and product. A common adult rheumatoid-arthritis regimen is 40 mg subcutaneously every other week; some patients not receiving methotrexate may require weekly dosing. Inflammatory bowel disease, hidradenitis, and some other indications use loading doses followed by maintenance therapy. Follow the exact product-specific regimen.
card.routeAdministration: Administer subcutaneously in the front of the thigh or abdomen, avoiding the area within 2 inches of the navel. Rotate sites and do not inject into skin that is tender, bruised, red, hard, scarred, or affected by psoriasis. Allow refrigerated medication to reach room temperature naturally, inspect the solution, and do not shake. Use each device once and discard it in a sharps container.
card.contraindications: Many current labels list no formal absolute contraindications. Do not initiate during an active serious infection, and avoid re-exposure after a serious hypersensitivity reaction to adalimumab or a product ingredient. Product-specific latex or excipient sensitivities must also be considered.
card.warnings: Boxed warning for serious infections and malignancy. Tuberculosis, invasive fungal infection, bacterial sepsis, opportunistic infection, hepatitis B reactivation, lymphoma, and other cancers may occur. Additional risks include demyelinating disease, severe cytopenias, worsening heart failure, lupus-like syndrome, severe allergy, and paradoxical psoriasis.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Injection-site redness, pain, swelling, or itching; upper respiratory infection; sinusitis; headache; rash; nausea; and mild laboratory abnormalities.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Tuberculosis; sepsis; invasive fungal or opportunistic infection; lymphoma, leukemia, hepatosplenic T-cell lymphoma, melanoma, or nonmelanoma skin cancer; hepatitis B reactivation; anaphylaxis; pancytopenia; demyelinating disease; severe liver injury; and new or worsening heart failure.
card.interactions: Avoid combining with other potent biologic immunosuppressants such as abatacept, anakinra, or another TNF blocker because serious infection risk increases. Live vaccines should not be given during treatment. Immunosuppressants, corticosteroids, and chemotherapy may further increase infection risk.
card.monitoring: Before therapy, screen for active and latent tuberculosis, hepatitis B, current infection, immunization status, heart failure, neurologic disease, and malignancy risk. During therapy, assess for fever, cough, weight loss, wounds, fungal exposure, hepatitis symptoms, neurologic changes, cytopenias, heart-failure symptoms, skin cancer, and treatment response. CBC and liver tests may be ordered.
card.patientTeaching: Do not inject when you have an active infection unless the prescriber approves. Report fever, persistent cough, night sweats, weight loss, shortness of breath, painful urination, wounds, shingles, jaundice, numbness, weakness, vision changes, unusual bruising, or new skin lesions. Learn correct injection and storage technique, never reuse devices, and discuss vaccines before treatment.
card.nutrition: No specific food restrictions are required. Maintain adequate protein, calories, and hydration during chronic inflammatory illness. Follow food-safety precautions if significantly immunosuppressed and promptly report poor intake, diarrhea, or weight loss that may indicate infection or active disease.

card.indications: Treatment of chronic hepatitis B in adults and pediatric patients 12 years and older with evidence of active viral replication and either persistent liver-enzyme elevation or histologic evidence of active liver disease.
card.mechanism: Converted inside cells to adefovir diphosphate, which competes with the natural nucleotide used by hepatitis B viral DNA polymerase. Incorporation terminates viral DNA-chain elongation and reduces HBV replication.
card.dosing: The usual dose for patients with normal renal function is 10 mg by mouth once daily. Renal impairment requires extending the dosing interval rather than increasing the dose. Hemodialysis patients require a product-specific schedule. Do not exceed the recommended dose because nephrotoxicity increases with exposure.
card.routeAdministration: Take once daily with or without food at a consistent time. Swallow the tablet with water. Do not stop treatment, skip prolonged periods, or run out without prescriber guidance because severe hepatitis flares may occur after discontinuation.
card.contraindications: Serious hypersensitivity to adefovir or a product ingredient. Do not use simultaneously with another adefovir-containing product. Avoid combining with tenofovir-containing products unless a specialist specifically determines otherwise because of overlapping antiviral therapy and renal toxicity concerns.
card.warnings: Boxed warnings include severe acute exacerbations of hepatitis after stopping therapy, nephrotoxicity, development of HIV resistance when unrecognized HIV infection is treated with adefovir alone, and lactic acidosis with severe hepatomegaly and steatosis. Renal injury and Fanconi syndrome may occur, especially in high-risk patients.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Weakness, headache, abdominal pain, nausea, flatulence, diarrhea, dyspepsia, and mild increases in creatinine. Patients with advanced liver disease may experience additional complications related to their illness.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Acute kidney injury, Fanconi syndrome, severe phosphate depletion, worsening hepatitis after discontinuation, hepatic decompensation, lactic acidosis, severe hepatomegaly with steatosis, and emergence of drug-resistant HIV in a patient with unrecognized HIV infection.
card.interactions: Other nephrotoxic drugs, including aminoglycosides, high-dose or multiple NSAIDs, cyclosporine, tacrolimus, cidofovir, and some antivirals, can increase renal injury. Drugs eliminated by active tubular secretion may compete for renal clearance. Avoid duplicate adefovir or inappropriate tenofovir-containing therapy.
card.monitoring: Test for HIV before starting and whenever clinically indicated. Obtain baseline and periodic serum creatinine, estimated renal function, and serum phosphorus in patients at risk. Monitor ALT, AST, bilirubin, HBV DNA, clinical response, and signs of hepatic decompensation. After discontinuation, monitor liver function closely for several months.
card.patientTeaching: Take every dose and do not stop without medical guidance. Keep laboratory appointments and do not allow the supply to run out. Report reduced urination, swelling, unusual weakness, muscle or bone pain, rapid breathing, severe nausea, abdominal swelling, jaundice, or worsening fatigue. Adefovir does not prevent transmission of hepatitis B; continue recommended precautions.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Maintain hydration unless restricted and avoid excessive alcohol because it can worsen liver injury. Do not start herbal products, bodybuilding supplements, or frequent NSAID use without review because of possible liver or kidney toxicity.

dosageForms: 6.25 mg, 12.5 mg, and 25 mg film-coated tablets

card.indications: Adjunct to diet and exercise to improve glycemic control in adults with type 2 diabetes mellitus. It is not used to treat type 1 diabetes or diabetic ketoacidosis.
card.mechanism: Inhibits dipeptidyl peptidase-4, prolonging the activity of incretin hormones such as GLP-1 and GIP. This increases glucose-dependent insulin release and decreases inappropriate glucagon secretion, helping lower fasting and post-meal glucose.
card.dosing: Usual dose: 25 mg by mouth once daily. Moderate renal impairment requires 12.5 mg once daily; severe renal impairment or end-stage renal disease requires 6.25 mg once daily. It may be given without regard to dialysis timing. Confirm renal function before selecting the dose.
card.routeAdministration: Take once daily with or without food. Swallow the tablet whole and do not split it when the product label directs against splitting. Give at the same time each day. If combined with insulin or a sulfonylurea, lower doses of those agents may be needed to reduce hypoglycemia risk.
card.contraindications: History of a serious hypersensitivity reaction to alogliptin or a product ingredient, including anaphylaxis, angioedema, or a severe cutaneous adverse reaction.
card.warnings: Acute pancreatitis, including severe cases, has been reported. Heart failure may occur or worsen, particularly in patients with cardiac or renal disease. Other major risks include serious hypersensitivity, hepatic injury, severe joint pain, bullous pemphigoid, and hypoglycemia when combined with insulin or an insulin secretagogue.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nasopharyngitis, headache, upper respiratory infection, mild gastrointestinal discomfort, and hypoglycemia when used with other glucose-lowering medications.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Acute pancreatitis; heart failure; anaphylaxis, angioedema, Stevens-Johnson syndrome, or other severe skin reaction; hepatic failure or serious liver-enzyme elevation; severe disabling joint pain; bullous pemphigoid; and severe hypoglycemia in combination therapy.
card.interactions: Alogliptin has relatively few major metabolic interactions, but insulin and sulfonylureas increase hypoglycemia risk. Other glucose-lowering medications may require adjustment. Review drugs that worsen heart failure, affect renal function, or mask hypoglycemia symptoms.
card.monitoring: Assess renal function before therapy and periodically, blood glucose, A1c, symptoms of hypo- or hyperglycemia, weight, edema, dyspnea, and heart-failure history. Monitor for persistent abdominal pain, nausea, liver-injury symptoms, severe joint pain, blisters, and allergic reactions. Liver tests are indicated when hepatic injury is suspected.
card.patientTeaching: Take daily even when you feel well and continue diet, exercise, glucose monitoring, and foot care. Report severe persistent abdominal pain, especially if it radiates to the back; shortness of breath; rapid weight gain; swelling; jaundice; dark urine; severe joint pain; blisters; facial swelling; or breathing difficulty. Know how to recognize and treat hypoglycemia when using combination therapy.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food. Follow the prescribed carbohydrate-controlled meal plan and avoid excessive alcohol, which can destabilize glucose and increase pancreatitis risk. Maintain hydration and do not skip meals when also using insulin or a sulfonylurea.

card.indications: Treatment of Parkinson disease symptoms and drug-induced extrapyramidal reactions. Some immediate-release labels also include influenza A prevention or treatment, but amantadine is not routinely recommended for current influenza management because resistance is widespread.
card.mechanism: The antiparkinson mechanism is not completely defined. Amantadine increases dopaminergic activity and has N-methyl-D-aspartate receptor antagonist effects, which can improve rigidity, tremor, bradykinesia, and dyskinesia. Its antiviral effect blocks the influenza A M2 ion channel.
card.dosing: Immediate-release dosing commonly begins at 100 mg once daily and may increase to 100 mg twice daily according to response and tolerance. Older adults and patients with reduced renal function require lower doses or longer intervals because the drug is cleared primarily by the kidneys. Extended-release products use different schedules and are not interchangeable.
card.routeAdministration: Give with or without food. Administer earlier in the day when possible if insomnia occurs. Measure oral solution accurately. Do not abruptly discontinue after prolonged use because severe worsening of Parkinsonism, delirium, agitation, or a neuroleptic-malignant-like syndrome may occur.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to amantadine or a product ingredient. Use extreme caution and individualized dosing in significant renal impairment, seizure disorders, psychosis, congestive heart failure, peripheral edema, and narrow-angle glaucoma.
card.warnings: Suicidal thoughts or attempts, hallucinations, psychosis, confusion, sudden sleep episodes, seizures, orthostatic hypotension, heart failure, peripheral edema, and impulse-control problems may occur. Abrupt withdrawal can cause severe neurologic deterioration or a neuroleptic-malignant-like syndrome. Accumulation from renal impairment can be fatal.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, dizziness, lightheadedness, insomnia, nervousness, dry mouth, constipation, headache, blurred vision, livedo reticularis, ankle edema, and mild confusion.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Suicidal behavior; severe hallucinations, delirium, or psychosis; seizures; dangerous arrhythmias; worsening heart failure; severe orthostatic hypotension or syncope; neuroleptic-malignant-like syndrome after withdrawal; corneal edema; and severe toxicity from renal accumulation.
card.interactions: Alcohol and other CNS-active drugs can worsen dizziness, confusion, or impaired judgment. Anticholinergic drugs increase dry mouth, constipation, urinary retention, blurred vision, and confusion. Quinidine, quinine, and some diuretic combinations may reduce renal clearance. Live attenuated intranasal influenza vaccine may be less effective when given near antiviral therapy.
card.monitoring: Assess renal function before and during therapy, especially in older adults. Monitor Parkinson or extrapyramidal symptoms, mental status, suicide risk, hallucinations, sleepiness, blood pressure lying and standing, edema, weight, heart-failure symptoms, seizure activity, vision changes, and impulse-control behavior.
card.patientTeaching: Do not stop suddenly. Avoid driving until alertness and vision effects are known, and avoid alcohol. Rise slowly. Report hallucinations, suicidal thoughts, unusual gambling or sexual urges, sudden sleep episodes, swelling, shortness of breath, fainting, seizure, severe confusion, or vision changes. Take missed doses only according to product instructions.
card.nutrition: May be taken with food to reduce nausea. Maintain fluids and fiber unless restricted because dry mouth and constipation are common. Avoid alcohol because it increases dizziness and confusion. Patients with heart failure should follow prescribed sodium and fluid limits and track weight and swelling.
